english: ceive, to know, to understand, dpyád-na ma rtógs-so (dpyad na ma rtogs so) they did not understand, though they inquired into it Dzl.; rtógs-par gyúr-ba (rtogs par gyur ba) to obtain information, to convince one's self of a thing Dzl.; rtógs-par byéd-pa (rtogs par byed pa) to teach, to demonstrate, to convince a person of Dzl.; má-rtógs-pa (ma rtogs pa) stupid, ignorant; ignorance Mil. — 2. sbst. (but in Tibetan always construed as an infinitive with the accus. inst. of the genit., and with an adv. inst. of an adj.) knowledge, perception, cognition, frq.; sems rtógs-pa (sems rtogs pa) the knowledge of (one's own) soul Mil.; mṅón-par rtógs-pa (mngon par rtogs pa) (अभिसमय) clear understanding or perception, in modern Buddhism the same as stoṅ-pa-ñid (stong pa nyid) Trig. 21. — rtógs-pa-čan (rtogs pa can), rtógs-ldán (rtogs ldan) rich in knowledge Mil. — rtógs-(pa) brjód(-pa) (rtogs (pa) brjod (pa)), for अपदान cf. Burn. I. 64, a moral legend. — rtogs-spyód (rtogs spyod) theory and practise, rtogs-spyód byéd-pa (rtogs spyod byed pa) to know and to do, rtogs-spyód la mkás-pa (rtogs spyod la mkhas pa) theoretically and practically religious. — rtógs-dod-čan (rtogs dod can) desirous of knowing or learning, inquisitive Mil. — Sometimes for togs-pa (togs pa).

wylie: rtod pa, gtod pa, btod pa
english: 1. sbst., also rtod-p'úr (rtod phur), a stake, in the ground, for teddering a horse, for securing a boat etc.; a peg, in a wall, for hanging up things; rtod-túg (rtod tug) a tedder (v. btod-pa (btod pa)); rtód-pa brgyáb-pa (rtod pa brgyab pa) to drive in a stake or peg. 2. vb. to tedder, fasten, secure Dzl.

wylie: rton pa, brton pa, brtan pa
english: with or without yid (yid), c. cd., to place confidence in a person, to rely on.
? čos-rtól (chos rtol) Tar. 164, 20, Schf. the pith or marrow of a doctrine; rtól-skyes-kyi śés-pa (rtol skyes kyi shes pa) Mil.? — brtól-śes-pa (brtol shes pa) Tar. 197, 8, Schf. to know thoroughly.

Cs., rtol-góg (rtol gog) Lex. w.e.; Sch.: a bastard, an animal of a mixed breed, rtól-po (rtol po) a male, rtól-mo (rtol mo) a female bastard Cs.; acc. to Desgodins the cross-breed of a yak-bull and a gar-mo (gar mo). Cf. ltor (ltor).

pf. brtol (brtol) (Ld. *stól-če* (*stol ce*)) 1. to bore, to pierce, to bore into, c. a. & t., Stg.; to bore through, to perforate c. a., a board etc., sgo-ṅa (sgo nga) the shell of an egg (of chickens creeping out) Sch., to open (an abscess) by a puncture; to make an incision; *bi-gaṅ* (*bi gang*) W. to bore a hole. — 2. to come to, to get to, to arrive at, gnás-su (gnas su) to (at) a place Lex. (cog. to t'ál-ba (thal ba), t'ól-ba (thol ba)); yoṅs-dus-brtol (yongs dus brtol) Lex. w.e.; Tar. 30, 22, Schf.: पारिवातक, the coral-tree, Erythrina indica; also a tree of paradise. (In Dzl. ༡༢༢, 13 the manuscript of Kyelang has: dé-dag-las rtól-ba (de dag las rtol ba) it out-passed them).

1. more correctly blta (blta), v. sub ltá-ba (lta ba), I. 1., we will see, Mil., frq. — 2. in various phrases and expressions, in which its special signification is no longer clearly discernible: a. lta ći smos (lta ci smos) Dzl. and elsewh., the most frq. form, lta smos ći dgos (lta smos ci dgos) Thgy., lta smos ći _tsal (lta smos ci 'tshal) (eleg.) Stg., W. more distinctly: *lta dgos ći yod* (*lta dgos ci yod*), also *zer dgos ći yod* (*zer dgos ci yod*), far from, not to mention, to say nothing of, how much less, how much more; with a preceding infinitive or noun: _di-dag _dul-ba lta ći smos ('di dag 'dul ba lta ci smos) to say nothing of the conversion of these! how much easier is it to convert these! Dzl.; _ó-skol lta ći smos ('o skol lta ci smos) how much more we! Thgy.; lta žog (lta zhog) is much the same; lo zlá-ba lta žog (lo zla ba lta zhog) to say nothing of years and months; *tar žog* (*tar zhog*), *tá-la žog* (*ta la zhog*) C. id. — b. the word is frq. used after participles or adjectives ending with pa (pa), when, judging in each case from the connection in which it happens to stand, it may be deemed equivalent to: evidently, indeed, thus then etc., spoken either with emphasis, or ironically, or in a sorrowful tone. As it is next to impossible to learn from the Tibetans the exact import of those little words, which slightly modify the grammatical and logical relations of a sentence, European translators have generally passed them over. Cf. Dzl. ༡༠༠, 18, ༡༤, 2 (where a shad ought to be added), ༤༢, 7 (where ste (ste) means though), ༡༤, 18; Tar. 7, 17, 19. In Dzl. ༡༢, 7 lta (lta), in accordance with the manuscript of Kyelang, is to be omitted. — c. like, as, (ltá-ba (lta ba) sbst. abstr., ltá-bu (lta bu) adj., ltá-bur (lta bur) or ltar (ltar) adv.), dú-bu ltá-bur yód- (du bu lta bur yod)
